g.data-narration: Welcome to Linux on Raspberry Pi for IoT Applications. Let's explore how to set up and manage Linux for IoT projects.
g.text: Linux on Raspberry Pi for IoT Applications
Operating System Setup, Command Line, GPIO Access, Package Management, and Security

g.data-narration: Linux is the preferred OS for Raspberry Pi IoT because it offers stability, security, open-source flexibility, and extensive hardware support.
g.text: Why Linux for IoT on Raspberry Pi?

g.data-narration: Raspberry Pi OS (formerly Raspbian) is a Debian-based Linux distribution optimized for Raspberry Pi hardware with a desktop or lite version.
g.text: Raspberry Pi OS - The Default Linux Distribution

g.data-narration: To install Linux, download the Raspberry Pi OS image and write it to an SD card using the Raspberry Pi Imager tool.
g.text: Setting Up Linux - Preparing the SD Card
Alternative tools: BalenaEtcher, dd command on Linux/Mac

g.data-narration: After first boot, configure your Pi by setting country preferences, changing the default password, connecting to WiFi, and updating the system.

g.text: Update system: sudo apt update && sudo apt upgrade

g.data-narration: For headless IoT devices, master essential commands: navigation (pwd, ls, cd), file operations (cp, mv, rm), and system monitoring (top, df -h).
g.text: pwd ls cd cp mv rm

g.data-narration: For IoT networking, use commands like ip addr for interface config, ping for connectivity testing, and nmcli for WiFi management.
Use apt for package management: apt update refreshes package lists, apt upgrade installs newer versions, and apt install adds new software.
g.text: Important: apt update vs upgrade - exam frequently tests this difference!

g.data-narration: For Python IoT development, use pip: pip3 install for system packages, and pip3 install --user for user-specific installations.
Use systemd and systemctl to manage services: start, stop, enable at boot, and check status of IoT daemons and applications.
Understand the Linux filesystem: /etc for config, /home/pi for user files, /var/log for logs, and /sys/class/gpio for hardware access.
Linux provides GPIO access through sysfs: export the pin, set direction (in/out), write value (0/1), and unexport when done.
Secure your IoT device: change default password, use SSH key authentication, enable firewall, and disable unnecessary services.
Make IoT apps auto-start at boot using systemd service files: create a .service file with [Unit], [Service], and [Install] sections.
